name: Nicholas Singleton
headline: Explosive rookie back stuck behind crowded Titans committee
body:
Singleton brings a genuine athletic pedigree to Tennessee after a productive Penn State career built on home-run speed and receiving chops, the kind of profile that flashes flex upside if the door opens. The problem in 2026 is the depth chart: he sits third in the backfield behind Tony Pollard and Tyjae Spears, who already split early-down and passing-down work. With no NFL advanced stats yet, his case rests on talent and draft pedigree rather than an established role.

The path to relevance is injury-dependent. Pollard has carried heavy workloads and Spears has missed time before, so one absence could vault Singleton into a real touch share, and his pass-catching skill means he would not be a pure early-down plodder. For now he is a bench stash with upside, not a draftable contributor. Treat him as a late-round dart in deeper formats and a strong dynasty stash given the age and pedigree, but do not bank on weekly production behind two veterans.
